title: Experimental Tabletop Petz recovery of a photonic qubit
authors: Hui Li, Jinyan Chen, Yue Pan, Liang Xu, Minjeong Song, Valerio Scarani, Lijian Zhang
abstract: The quantum information lost in open evolutions cannot be fully recovered, but partial recovery is possible. The Petz recovery map guarantees almost optimal recovery, notably if the chosen reference state is close to the real one. This map has been widely used in theoretical studies, but has been the object of only a handful of experimental realisations, typically under a single fixed noise model. In this work, we describe and implement the Petz recovery map for a versatile class of qubit channels with tunable decoherence and dissipation. The setup we realize is also the first experimental example of ``tabletop reversibility'': for a good range of choices of the reference state, the Petz recovery map can be implemented with the same devices as the forward dissipative evolution, whose effect it is partially undoing. Our results demonstrate that the Petz recovery map can be resource-efficiently realized without requiring complex ancillary resources, providing a feasible pathway for mitigating information loss in quantum systems.
summary: The paper experimentally realizes the Petz recovery map for photonic qubits to combat information loss from decoherence. By showing that this recovery process can be implemented using the same physical hardware as the noise itself, they demonstrate a resource-efficient method for mitigating quantum information decay in tabletop setups.
main_problem: The work addresses the challenge of partially recovering quantum information lost during open system evolution (decoherence/dissipation) using the Petz recovery map.
main_result: They experimentally demonstrated 'tabletop reversibility,' showing that the Petz recovery map can be implemented using the same photonic devices as the forward dissipative evolution, resource-efficiently mitigating information loss.
method: The authors used spontaneous parametric down-conversion to generate photonic qubits and employed state tomography alongside fidelity/trace distance measurements to validate the channel reconstruction.
model_or_system: The system uses photonic qubits encoded in photon polarization. The dynamics are modeled by CPTP quantum channels, including tunable decoherence and dissipation components.
key_observables: State Fidelity ($F$) and Trace Distance ($T$), used to quantify the success of information recovery for various input states ($ho_H, ho_V, ho_D, ho_R$).
important_parameters: Forward channel parameters $(p, s, 	heta, \kappa, \lambda)$ and the reference state parameter $r$. The operational range for $r$ is constrained by theoretical derivations.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the Petz recovery map can be mathematically mapped onto a structure analogous to the forward channel, allowing for physical implementation using existing hardware components.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically compares the forward and recovery channels. Figure 2 details the experimental setup (state prep, forward channel, recovery map). Figure 3 shows Bloch sphere trajectories and fidelity across different reference states. Table I quantifies performance metrics.
paper_structure: The paper moves from theoretical derivation of the Petz map structure using Choi matrices to establishing parameter relationships, culminating in an experimental realization demonstrating that the required channels can be implemented with minimal additional resources.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, resource-efficient experimental platform for implementing quantum error mitigation techniques (like partial recovery) directly on photonic qubits, which is highly relevant for scalable quantum communication and computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram illustrating the forward channel and the corresponding Petz recovery map acting as the backward channel. Both channels consist of three parts: identity channel I, rotation channel Rrot and the dissipation channel Dκ,λ.

FIG. 2. Experimental setup for implementing the forward channel and the Petz recovery map. The setup contains four parts: (a) state preparation, (b) forward channel, (c) Petz recovery map and (d) state tomography. Experimentally, the photons are generated via the spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) and collected by the APD. The identity part I and the rotation part Rπ/2 rot (Rθ rot) of the forward channel (Petz recovery map) are combined and implemented as a single new rotation operation Rα rot (Rα′ rot) (see Section D for detailed derivation). The values of κ(′), λ(′) and α(′) are determined by adjusting the angle of the corresponding HWP and the thickness of the PR,...

FIG. 3. Experimental performance of the Petz recovery map. (A) Recovery trajectories on the Bloch sphere for the reference state σ2. Only σ2 is shown here, for simplicity, as maps corresponding to other reference states (σ1, σ3, σ4, σ5) exhibit similar trajectories. (B) Recovery fidelity F of various input states across different Petz recovery map for different reference states. In both panels, circles with different colors distinguish the input states (ρH, ρV, ρD, ρR) with the star specifically indicating the reference state. Squares with dashed lines and triangles with solid lines represent the theoretical and experimental recovered states, respectively. Error bars in panel B are smaller...
